1: Psalm 16 carries the superscription miktam — an uncertain genre term appearing in Pss 16 and 56-60. Proposed meanings include a "golden poem" (kātam = gold) or a penitential form, but no consensus exists. The psalm is centrally important for early Christian theology: Peter cites Psalms 16:8-11 at Pentecost (Acts 2:25-28) and Paul cites Psalms 16:10 at Pisidian Antioch (Acts 13:35), both applying "your Holy One will not see decay" to Jesus's resurrection. The opening "keep me safe (šāmrēnî) for in you I take shelter (ḥāsîtî)" establishes the covenantal posture of complete dependence — ḥāsāh (take refuge) is the root of maḥseh (refuge), the Psalter's standard term for divine protection.
2: "I said to the LORD, 'You are my Lord (ʾādonāy); I have no good apart from you.'" — the radical covenant confession: God is the source of all good, not only religious or spiritual good. ʾĀdonāy (my Lord) is the respectful vocative form used for YHWH. "My good is nothing without you (ṭôbātî bal-ʿāleykā)" is textually and grammatically debated (the LXX reads it differently), but in context expresses absolute dependence: all benefit flows from the relationship with YHWH; outside it there is no ṭôb (good, flourishing).
3: "As for the holy ones who are in the land, they are the noble — in them is all my delight" — this verse is textually difficult. The most natural reading: the qĕdôšîm (holy ones) are the covenant faithful in Israel, the pious community. Their nobility (ʾaddîrê) is not social rank but spiritual standing. The psalmist's delight (ḥēpeṣ) in the community of the faithful mirrors YHWH's own delight in the righteous (Psalms 147:11) — the psalmist's values align with God's, forming a coherent pattern of covenant loyalty at both the vertical and horizontal dimensions.
4: "The sorrows of those who chase other gods will multiply" — the covenant warning of Deut 28-30: apostasy produces suffering. ʿAṣbôtām (sorrows, idols — the same root as ʿōṣeḇ, idol) may be a deliberate wordplay: both their idols and their sorrows will multiply. "I will not pour out their libations of blood" — blood libations were Canaanite or foreign ritual practices; Israelite law strictly prohibited the drinking of blood (Leviticus 17:10-14) and blood-ritual worship. The psalmist refuses cultic assimilation to surrounding practices, maintaining covenant distinctiveness.
5: "The LORD is my allotted portion (mĕnāt-ḥelqî) and my cup (kôsî)" — two layered images. (1) Ḥēleq (portion, allotment): the Levites received no tribal land inheritance because YHWH was their portion (Numbers 18:20; Deuteronomy 10:9; Psalms 18:2) — to be sustained by the altar, not the earth. The psalmist claims the same priestly relationship: not land but YHWH himself is the inheritance. (2) The cup: in covenant ritual, the cup of blessing was passed at sacred meals (cf. 1 Corinthians 10:16); the cup could also contain wrath (Psalms 11:6; Psalms 75:8; Isaiah 51:17). Here the cup is blessing.
6: "The boundary lines have fallen for me in pleasant places; truly I have a beautiful inheritance" — land allotment language. When Joshua divided Canaan by lot, each tribe received its ḥēleq (inheritance), the boundary lines (ḥăbālîm) marking the permanent grant (Josh 18-19). The psalmist applies this to his relationship with God: the "inheritance" of divine fellowship is more desirable than any parcel of land. The contentment expressed — "pleasant places (nĕʿîmôt)," "beautiful inheritance (šāpĕrāh ʿālay)" — echoes the šālôm of Psalms 4:7-8: satisfaction from the divine presence itself rather than material provision.
7: "I will praise the LORD who counsels me (yĕʿāṣênî); even at night my inmost self (kĕlāyôtay — kidneys) instructs me" — divine counsel received in the night connects Ps 16 with Ps 17 (also a night vigil). The kidneys (kĕlāyôt) were the seat of deep feeling and moral discernment in ancient Israelite anthropology (cf. Jeremiah 11:20; Psalms 17:10; Psalms 20:12 — where God examines the kidneys and heart), analogous to the modern conscience or deepest intuition. Divine instruction reaches the deepest level of the person, not merely the conscious surface.
8: "I have set the LORD always before me; because he is at my right hand (liymînî), I will not be shaken" — the right hand is the position of the chief advocate, counselor, or protector (cf. Psalms 110:1 — God says to the Davidic lord: "sit at my right hand"). The king's military commander stood at his right hand. Peter at Pentecost (Acts 2:25) quotes this verse and the following as David speaking prophetically of Christ — the one who is now literally exalted to God's right hand (Acts 2:33). The covenant-stability phrase "will not be shaken" recurs across Pss 10-21.
9: "My heart is glad and my spirit (kābôd, lit. glory/honor-self) rejoices; my body (bāśār, flesh) also rests secure" — the triad of heart (lēb), glory-self (kābôd), and body (bāśār) expresses total well-being. Using kābôd for the inner self is unusual — possibly the psalmist's social-spiritual honor-identity. The LXX translates kābôd as glōssa (tongue), a significant deviation that shapes Peter's quotation in Acts 2:26 ("therefore my heart was glad, and my tongue rejoiced"). The Hebrew triad and the LXX triad each emphasize totality of wellbeing.
10: "For you will not leave my soul (nafšî) in Sheol, or let your Holy One (ḥăsîdĕkā) see decay (šaḥat)" — the center of the NT's reading of Ps 16. Peter (Acts 2:27-31) and Paul (Acts 13:35-37) argue that David cannot be speaking only of himself, since David did die and his tomb was known in Jerusalem; therefore, David spoke prophetically of Christ's resurrection as the fulfillment of the non-decaying Holy One. Šaḥat (pit/decay) — whether it means the pit of Sheol or physical decomposition carries significant interpretive weight. The psalm originally expresses personal confidence in divine preservation from death; the NT reads the promise as pointing beyond any Davidic fulfillment.
11: "You will make known to me the path of life (ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm); in your presence is complete joy (śimbat pānîm); at your right hand are pleasures forevermore (nĕʿimôt niṣṣaḥ)" — Ōraḥ ḥayyîm (path of life) is wisdom language: the way that leads away from death (Proverbs 2:19; Psalms 5:6; Psalms 10:17). The complete joy in God's presence contrasts with the multiplied sorrows of those who chase other gods (Psalms 16:4). "Pleasures forevermore" is an eschatological horizon — joy in God's presence has no terminus. The psalm closes not at Sheol but at the right hand of God, where the fullness of joy and eternal pleasures reside.
